title: Rainbow Hats

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: 7 prisoners are each given a hat in one of 7 'rainbow' colors (the executioner chooses the colors arbitrarily, and colors can repeat). Each prisoner can see the other 6 hats but not their own. Each prisoner writes down a guess for their own hat color simultaneously -- no communication.
rules: Prisoners can plan a strategy together before any hats are placed.
Once hats are placed, no communication is allowed.
Each prisoner writes ONE guess for their own hat color.
If AT LEAST ONE prisoner guesses correctly, ALL 7 go free.
setup.question: Design a guaranteed strategy that always saves all 7 prisoners.

learningObjectives: Recognize that each prisoner can claim a unique 'residue class' so that the 7 guesses cover all possible sums-mod-7.
Use proof by contradiction to show at least one prisoner is correct: if all were wrong, the true sum's residue would fit no class.
See the deep connection between modular arithmetic and proof by contradiction.

stages.label: Why naive strategies fail
stages.prompt: Suppose each prisoner guesses a fixed color. What's the worst case?

options.text: All 7 could be wrong if the executioner picks colors that don't match any of the fixed guesses.
At least one is always correct.

stages.explanation: Independent guessing has no guarantee. We need a strategy where the 7 guesses COLLECTIVELY cover all possibilities.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Assign color codes 0-6 and prisoner labels 0-6. Each prisoner i guesses the color making (sum of all 7 hats) = i mod 7. The 7 prisoners cover all 7 residue classes -- one MUST be correct.

stages.explanation: Modular arithmetic + proof by contradiction. Each prisoner claims a unique residue class. Whatever the actual sum's residue is, the prisoner who claims that class guesses correctly. The proof that this works is by contradiction.

stages.label: The strategy
stages.prompt: Prisoner i sees the other 6 hats. Let s_i = sum of their codes. What does prisoner i guess for their own hat color?

options.text: g_i = i - s_i mod 7. So that g_i + s_i = i mod 7 -- making the TOTAL hat sum equal to i mod 7 IF prisoner i's hat is g_i.

stages.explanation: Prisoner i computes g_i = (i - s_i) mod 7. If their actual hat code is g_i, then the total sum (g_i + s_i) = i mod 7 -- and prisoner i guesses correctly. Each prisoner is 'responsible for' the case where the true total is congruent to their own label.

stages.label: Proof by contradiction
stages.prompt: Suppose ALL 7 prisoners guess wrongly. What goes wrong?

options.text: If everyone is wrong, the true sum's residue mod 7 matches NONE of {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. But every integer's residue mod 7 IS in that set -- contradiction.
Two prisoners guess the same color.
The strategy works only on average.

stages.explanation: Contradiction: every integer modulo 7 has SOME residue in {0..6}. So whatever the true sum is, exactly one prisoner -- the one whose label equals that residue -- guesses correctly. 'All wrong' is impossible.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: Does the strategy guarantee all 7 prisoners go free?

options.text: Yes -- exactly one prisoner ALWAYS guesses correctly (the one whose label equals the true residue).

stages.explanation: Exactly one prisoner is always correct -- the one whose label matches the true residue mod 7. Since 'at least one correct' triggers freedom, all 7 always go free.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why does the strategy require exactly N = 7 prisoners and N = 7 colors? What if there were 8 colors?
How is this connected to the Prisoner Hat problem (Section 2.7) where one prisoner SACRIFICES?
Can you design a similar 'covers all residues' strategy for 100 prisoners with 100 colors?
